rationale: The citing paper uses Herzenstein, Posavac, and Brakus (2007) to justify a core theoretical premise: perceived risk is a key barrier to adoption of really new products, which directly supports its H4 risk-mediation hypotheses. It also cites that paper as the source for its perceived newness manipulation-check items (e.g., "new" and "novel"), indicating methodological borrowing. The reference appears in hypothesis development, method, and discussion, so it plays a meaningful role beyond a passing literature-list mention.
relation_description: The 2015 paper builds on prior evidence from Herzenstein et al. that risk salience and uncertainty shape adoption of really new products, using this as a basis for its own risk-based mechanism. It also adopts measurement elements from that study for perceived innovation newness checks.
